Hands-on Exercise 5: Visualising Multivariate Data

Author

Meng Tingyu

Published

May 15, 2026

Modified

May 15, 2026

1 Learning Journey Overview

Welcome to my fifth hands-on exercise. This time the focus is squarely on multivariate data, which is what most real datasets look like once we stop pretending the world has only two dimensions. Across the five sections below, I work through five complementary techniques, each suited to a slightly different analytical question:

  1. Ternary plots for three-part compositional data (Chapter 13).
  2. Correlation visualisation for pairwise relationships among many numerical variables (Chapter 6).
  3. Heatmaps for spotting clusters and patterns across an entire matrix at once (Chapter 14).
  4. Parallel coordinates plots for tracing how individual observations behave across many variables (Chapter 15).
  5. Treemaps for hierarchical part-to-whole composition (Chapter 16).

2 Part 1: Ternary Plots for Compositional Data

2.1 What is a ternary plot, intuitively?

A ternary plot is a triangle in which every point inside represents a three-part composition that sums to 100%. Each side of the triangle is one component, and the position of the point tells me how that observation splits between the three. The classic textbook example is soil composition (sand, silt, clay), but here I use it for Singapore’s population structure: young, economically active, and old.

The reason a ternary plot beats three separate bar charts is that the three proportions are constrained: if young goes up, one of the others must come down. The triangle visualises that trade-off directly.

2.2 Setting up the packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)

2.3 Importing and preparing the data

pop_data <- read_csv("data/respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv")

The raw data has age groups in long format, but for a ternary plot I need exactly three numbers per row. So I bucket the age groups into YOUNG, ACTIVE, and OLD, then keep only 2018.

agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  mutate(`Year` = as.character(Year)) %>%
  spread(AG, Population) %>%
  mutate(YOUNG = rowSums(.[4:8])) %>%
  mutate(ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16])) %>%
  mutate(OLD = rowSums(.[17:21])) %>%
  mutate(TOTAL = rowSums(.[22:24])) %>%
  filter(Year == 2018) %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

What is actually happening here:

  • spread() pivots age groups from rows to columns so I can sum them by category.
  • rowSums(.[4:8]) is a compact way to add specific columns by position. It works, but it is fragile if column order ever changes, so in production code I would probably use select() plus column names instead.
  • The final filter(TOTAL > 0) removes subzones with no residents, which would otherwise be undefined on the triangle.

2.4 Building a static ternary plot

ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, 
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Population structure, 2018") +
  theme_rgbw()

theme_rgbw() gives the triangle a clean white background with light gridlines, which I find much easier to read than the default. The ggtern API uses x, y, and z instead of two axes, but otherwise everything composes like normal ggplot layers.

2.5 Building an interactive ternary plot

For the interactive version I switch to plot_ly() with type = "scatterternary". The interactivity is genuinely useful here because each point is a planning subzone, and being able to hover to identify which subzone has an unusually old or young profile is much more efficient than reading a legend.

label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt, 
    x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper", 
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2
  )
}

axis <- function(txt) {
  list(
    title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10)
  )
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"), 
  baxis = axis("Active"), 
  caxis = axis("Old")
)

plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated, 
  a = ~YOUNG, 
  b = ~ACTIVE, 
  c = ~OLD, 
  color = I("black"), 
  type = "scatterternary"
) %>%
  layout(
    annotations = label("Ternary Markers"), 
    ternary = ternaryAxes
  )

2.6 My takeaway

Ternary plots are surprisingly powerful when the data really is compositional. And They only make sense when the three parts logically sum to a whole. For Singapore’s age structure, the cluster of points pulled towards the “active” corner tells the demographic story in one glance.

3 Part 2: Visual Correlation Analysis

3.1 Why bother visualising correlations?

When I have ten or more numerical variables, eyeballing a correlation table of numbers is exhausting and error-prone. A visual correlation matrix solves three problems at once:

  1. It reveals the pairwise relationships between variables at a glance.
  2. It feeds into downstream analyses such as regression and factor analysis.
  3. It acts as a diagnostic for multicollinearity before I run any model.

For this section I use the Wine Quality dataset, with 11 numerical chemical properties across roughly 6,500 wines.

3.2 Packages

pacman::p_load(corrplot, ggstatsplot, tidyverse)

3.3 Importing the data

wine <- read_csv("data/wine_quality.csv")

3.4 Method 1: Base R pairs()

The simplest scatterplot matrix uses base R’s pairs():

pairs(wine[, 2:12])

Since a correlation matrix is symmetric, showing both halves is redundant. I prefer to show only the lower half:

pairs(wine[, 2:12], upper.panel = NULL)

And to be smarter still, I can replace one half with the actual correlation coefficients, sized by magnitude:

panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits = 2, prefix = "", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr")
  on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits = digits)[1]
  txt <- paste(prefix, txt, sep = "")
  if (missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8 / strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}

pairs(wine[, 2:12], upper.panel = panel.cor)
NoteWhen pairs() breaks down

With 6,500 observations the scatter clouds become huge inky blobs and stop being readable. That is exactly the limitation that motivates the corrgram approach below.

3.5 Method 2: ggcorrmat() from ggstatsplot

ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat() is my favourite for reporting, because it produces a publication-ready figure with statistical annotation baked in:

ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = wine, 
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black", 
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
  subtitle = "Four pairs are not significant at p < 0.05"
)

hc.order = TRUE reorders the variables by hierarchical clustering, similar variables end up next to each other, so the block structure of the correlation matrix becomes visually obvious.

For grouped comparisons (red wine vs white wine), I can use grouped_ggcorrmat():

grouped_ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  grouping.var = type,
  type = "robust",
  p.adjust.method = "holm",
  plotgrid.args = list(ncol = 2),
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black", 
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  annotation.args = list(
    tag_levels = "a",
    title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
    caption = "Dataset: UCI Machine Learning Repository"
  )
)

3.6 Method 3: corrplot for fine control

Where ggcorrmat is opinionated, corrplot is endlessly customisable. First I compute the correlation matrix explicitly:

wine.cor <- cor(wine[, 1:11])
corrplot(wine.cor)

The default uses circles, with blue for positive and red for negative correlations, and colour intensity for magnitude.

3.6.1 Changing the visual encoding

Seven method options exist (circle, square, ellipse, number, shade, color, pie). Ellipses are my go-to because the tilt of the ellipse encodes the sign of the correlation visually:

corrplot(wine.cor, 
         method = "ellipse",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black")

3.6.2 Mixed layout: glyphs on one side, numbers on the other

This is the design I usually settle on for final reports because it captures both shape and exact magnitude in one figure:

corrplot.mixed(wine.cor, 
               lower = "ellipse", 
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               tl.col = "black")

3.6.3 Significance testing overlay

Correlation strength is not the whole story.

wine.sig = cor.mtest(wine.cor, conf.level = .95)

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "number",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.srt = 45,
         p.mat = wine.sig$p,
         sig.level = .05)

Non-significant cells get crossed out, which is much more informative than presenting raw r-values as if they were all equally trustworthy.

3.6.4 Reordering with hierarchical clustering

Reordering is where corrgrams really earn their keep. By default the variables appear in whatever order the dataframe had them, which is rarely the most informative arrangement:

corrplot(wine.cor, 
         method = "ellipse", 
         tl.pos = "lt",
         tl.col = "black",
         order = "hclust",
         hclust.method = "ward.D",
         addrect = 3)

The addrect = 3 argument draws boxes around three clusters.

3.7 My takeaway

For exploration I reach for corrplot because of its flexibility. For formal reports I prefer ggcorrmat because the statistical annotation is built in. Either way, reordering by clustering is the single most underrated technique here, it transforms a chaotic matrix into a structured one.

4 Part 3: Heatmaps for Multivariate Patterns

4.1 What heatmaps are good at

A heatmap is essentially a coloured table. Rows are observations, columns are variables, and each cell’s colour encodes a value. The reason this works so well is that human vision is very good at picking out horizontal bands of similar colour (similar observations) and vertical bands (related variables). Adding dendrograms on the sides lets the algorithm reorder both axes so those bands actually emerge.

For this section I use the World Happiness 2018 dataset.

4.2 Packages

pacman::p_load(seriation, dendextend, heatmaply, tidyverse)

4.3 Importing and preparing the data

wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")
row.names(wh) <- wh$Country

Setting row names to country names is essential, otherwise the heatmap labels are useless row numbers. The next step is converting to a matrix because most heatmap functions require that input class:

wh_matrix <- data.matrix(wh)

4.4 Static heatmap with heatmap()

The base R heatmap() is a fine starting point:

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)

With Rowv = NA and Colv = NA, both dendrograms are switched off, so I see the raw matrix with no reordering. That is rarely what I want, but it is useful as a baseline.

4.4.1 The scaling problem

If I plot the matrix without scaling, one variable (Happiness Score) dominates because its raw values are much larger than the others. Everything else looks washed out. Column-wise scaling fixes this:

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      scale = "column",
                      cexRow = 0.6, 
                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))

scale = "column" z-scores each column, so every variable now contributes on equal footing. This is a small change with an enormous effect on interpretability.

4.5 Interactive heatmap with heatmaply

heatmaply produces interactive heatmaps that render as standalone HTML.

heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)])

4.5.1 Three data transformation methods

heatmaply offers three transformations, and choosing between them is more than cosmetic:

# Scaling: assumes (or imposes) normal-like distributions
heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)],
          scale = "column")

# Normalising: maps to [0, 1] using min and max
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))

# Percentising: maps each value to its empirical percentile
heatmaply(percentize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))
  • Scale when variables are roughly normal and outliers are meaningful.
  • Normalise when distributions are skewed but I still want raw shape preserved.
  • Percentise when I care about relative rank rather than absolute values, which is often the case for indicators across countries.

4.5.2 Picking the right clustering setup

Choosing a clustering method by intuition feels arbitrary. The dendextend package lets me pick statistically:

wh_d <- dist(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), method = "euclidean")
dend_expend(wh_d)[[3]]

The output table shows an optim score for each clustering method. The highest score wins, which in this dataset is average. Then I find the best number of clusters:

wh_clust <- hclust(wh_d, method = "average")
num_k <- find_k(wh_clust)
plot(num_k)

Putting both choices into the heatmap call:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "average",
          k_row = 3)

4.5.3 Seriation: getting the row order right

Clustering tells me the tree structure, but it does not tell me how to rotate the branches. Two valid orderings of the same cluster tree can produce very different-looking heatmaps. The seriate = "OLO" (Optimal Leaf Ordering) argument fixes this by minimising the sum of dissimilarities between adjacent rows:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          seriate = "OLO")

4.5.4 Final polished version

Putting it all together with thoughtful styling:

heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          Colv = NA,
          seriate = "none",
          colors = Blues,
          k_row = 5,
          margins = c(NA, 200, 60, NA),
          fontsize_row = 4,
          fontsize_col = 5,
          main = "World Happiness Score and Variables by Country, 2018",
          xlab = "World Happiness Indicators",
          ylab = "World Countries")

4.6 My takeaway

Heatmaps reward attention to three details: scaling, clustering choice, and leaf ordering. Get all three right and the patterns essentially announce themselves. Get any one wrong and the chart can mislead.

5 Part 4: Parallel Coordinates Plots

5.1 What parallel coordinates plots do differently

If a scatterplot matrix shows me pairwise relationships, a parallel coordinates plot shows me whole observations as lines across many variables. Each vertical axis is one variable. Each line is one observation, threading through all the variables in sequence. This is uniquely good at:

  • Identifying clusters of similarly-behaved observations.
  • Spotting outliers that take an unusual path across variables.
  • Characterising customer segments or country groupings.

5.2 Packages

pacman::p_load(GGally, parallelPlot, tidyverse)

5.3 Importing the data

wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")

5.4 Static plot with ggparcoord()

A bare-minimum plot:

ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12))

This is honestly not very useful on its own. Without colour or scaling, all lines pile on top of each other. The makeover below is where it becomes informative:

ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12), 
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE, 
           title = "Parallel Coordinates Plot of World Happiness Variables")

The four arguments that transform this chart:

  • groupColumn = 2 colours lines by Region, which is where regional patterns start to emerge.
  • scale = "uniminmax" puts every axis on a 0-to-1 range, so axes are comparable.
  • alphaLines = 0.2 makes lines semi-transparent, so overlapping bundles look denser. This is essential for any dataset above maybe 30 observations.
  • boxplot = TRUE overlays a boxplot per axis, summarising distribution shape.

5.4.1 Faceting by region

For comparing regions head-to-head, faceting works beautifully:

ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12), 
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE, 
           title = "Parallel Coordinates by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

The angle = 30, hjust = 1 combination rotates and right-aligns axis labels so they do not overlap the plot, which is a small detail that makes a big visual difference.

5.5 Interactive plot with parallelPlot

For exploratory work, interactivity is genuinely useful here because clicking on an axis recolours all lines by that variable, which is the fastest way to ask “what drives this variable?”:

wh <- wh %>%
  select("Happiness score", c(7:12))

parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle = TRUE)

5.5.1 Customising colour and adding histograms

parallelPlot(wh,
             continuousCS = "YlOrRd",
             rotateTitle = TRUE)

histoVisibility <- rep(TRUE, ncol(wh))
parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle = TRUE,
             histoVisibility = histoVisibility)

The histograms along each axis are an underrated feature, because they show me the marginal distribution of each variable without leaving the chart.

5.6 My takeaway

Parallel coordinates plots have a steep aesthetic learning curve. The defaults look terrible. But once I add scaling, transparency, grouping colour, and boxplots, they become one of the most informative views possible for medium-sized multivariate datasets. The interactive version is what I would actually use in a live exploratory session.

6 Part 5: Treemap for Hierarchical Composition

6.1 When to use a treemap

Treemaps shine for hierarchical part-to-whole data where I care about both the level of the hierarchy and the relative sizes of leaves. Classic use cases:

  • Budget breakdowns by department and team.
  • Stock market sectors and constituent companies.
  • Property transactions by region, planning area, and project (which is exactly what I do here).

The two things I will always encode are rectangle size (a magnitude) and rectangle colour (a separate metric, often a rate or median).

6.2 Packages

pacman::p_load(treemap, treemapify, tidyverse)

6.3 Data wrangling

The raw REALIS 2018 dataset is at the transaction level. A treemap needs aggregated values, so I group and summarise:

realis2018 <- read_csv("data/realis2018.csv")

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>% 
  group_by(`Project Name`, `Planning Region`, 
           `Planning Area`, `Property Type`, 
           `Type of Sale`) %>%
  summarise(`Total Unit Sold` = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE), 
            `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Transacted Price` = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))

Notice the pipe pattern: group, then summarise. This is one of those dplyr workflows that you internalise once and then use forever. The na.rm = TRUE is essential because any missing value in a column would otherwise propagate to the whole summary.

Filter to a single property type and sale type to keep the example focused:

realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(`Property Type` == "Condominium", 
         `Type of Sale` == "Resale")

6.4 Static treemap with treemap()

The three arguments that do most of the work are index, vSize, and vColor:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
WarningA subtle gotcha

By default, vColor does not actually map to a colour gradient. Without setting type, treemap() assumes type = "index" and colours rectangles by hierarchy category instead of by the numeric variable.

6.4.1 Fixing the colour mapping

To actually map colour to the numeric variable, I need to set type:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "value",
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

6.4.2 “value” vs “manual”: what is the difference?

This tripped me up at first. Both use vColor, but the mapping logic differs:

  • type = "value" treats the palette as diverging around zero. Negative values pull towards the left-end colour, positive values pull towards the right. Good for data that can be negative.
  • type = "manual" maps min(values) to the left end and max(values) to the right end, with mean in the middle. Good for strictly-positive data.

For unit prices, which are all positive, manual with a sequential palette like Blues is the right combination:

treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "manual",
        palette = "Blues", 
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

6.4.3 Layout algorithms

treemap supports two algorithms:

  • squarified: optimises aspect ratios so rectangles are close to squares. Ignores sort order.
  • pivotSize (default): respects a sortID ordering at the cost of slightly worse aspect ratios.
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "manual",
        palette = "Blues", 
        algorithm = "pivotSize",
        sortID = "Median Transacted Price",
        title = "Sorted by Median Transacted Price")

I usually pick squarified for purely visual comparison and pivotSize with sortID when there is a meaningful order to encode.

6.5 Treemap with treemapify (ggplot grammar)

treemapify lets me build treemaps inside ggplot, which is great when I want to combine with other ggplot features:

ggplot(data = realis2018_selected, 
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) + 
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "gray20")

The two *_border layers draw nested borders for each hierarchy level, which makes the structure visually obvious. I find this version cleaner than the treemap() output for final reports.

6.6 Interactive treemap with d3treeR

For drilldown exploration, the interactive d3treeR package wraps a treemap object into a clickable d3 visualisation:

# install.packages("devtools")
# library(devtools)
# install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")
library(d3treeR)
tm <- treemap(realis2018_summarised,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "value",
        title = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")

Clicking a region zooms into that region’s planning areas. For a stakeholder-facing dashboard this is genuinely useful, because it lets the viewer explore at their own pace instead of forcing one fixed view.

6.7 My takeaway

Treemaps are easy to make wrong and reasonably easy to make right. The two non-negotiables are: (1) set type explicitly so colour actually maps to your numeric variable, and (2) use a sequential palette when values are strictly positive. Everything else is polish.

7 Closing Reflections

Chart type Best for Watch out for
Ternary plot Three-part compositions Only works if parts sum to a whole
Correlation visualisation Pairwise numerical relationships Reorder by clustering, always
Heatmap Multivariate patterns and clusters Scale your data, choose seriation carefully
Parallel coordinates Tracing observations across variables Use transparency and grouping colour
Treemap Hierarchical part-to-whole Set type explicitly, pick palette to match data sign

What unifies them is that each one is a different answer to the same question: how do I see structure in a dataset that has more than two or three dimensions?

Source Code
---
title: "Hands-on Exercise 5: Visualising Multivariate Data"
author: "Meng Tingyu"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
date-modified: "last-modified"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    code-copy: true
    theme: cosmo
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 6
    fig-align: center
    smooth-scroll: true
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: true
editor: visual
---

# Learning Journey Overview

Welcome to my fifth hands-on exercise. This time the focus is squarely on **multivariate data**, which is what most real datasets look like once we stop pretending the world has only two dimensions. Across the five sections below, I work through five complementary techniques, each suited to a slightly different analytical question:

1.  **Ternary plots** for three-part compositional data (Chapter 13).
2.  **Correlation visualisation** for pairwise relationships among many numerical variables (Chapter 6).
3.  **Heatmaps** for spotting clusters and patterns across an entire matrix at once (Chapter 14).
4.  **Parallel coordinates plots** for tracing how individual observations behave across many variables (Chapter 15).
5.  **Treemaps** for hierarchical part-to-whole composition (Chapter 16).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 1: Ternary Plots for Compositional Data

## What is a ternary plot, intuitively?

A ternary plot is a triangle in which every point inside represents a **three-part composition that sums to 100%**. Each side of the triangle is one component, and the position of the point tells me how that observation splits between the three. The classic textbook example is soil composition (sand, silt, clay), but here I use it for Singapore's population structure: young, economically active, and old.

The reason a ternary plot beats three separate bar charts is that the three proportions are *constrained*: if young goes up, one of the others must come down. The triangle visualises that trade-off directly.

## Setting up the packages

```{r}
#| label: ternary-setup
pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)
```

## Importing and preparing the data

```{r}
#| label: ternary-import
#| eval: false
pop_data <- read_csv("data/respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv")
```

The raw data has age groups in long format, but for a ternary plot I need exactly three numbers per row. So I bucket the age groups into `YOUNG`, `ACTIVE`, and `OLD`, then keep only 2018.

```{r}
#| label: ternary-mutate
#| eval: false
agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  mutate(`Year` = as.character(Year)) %>%
  spread(AG, Population) %>%
  mutate(YOUNG = rowSums(.[4:8])) %>%
  mutate(ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16])) %>%
  mutate(OLD = rowSums(.[17:21])) %>%
  mutate(TOTAL = rowSums(.[22:24])) %>%
  filter(Year == 2018) %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)
```

What is actually happening here:

-   `spread()` pivots age groups from rows to columns so I can sum them by category.
-   `rowSums(.[4:8])` is a compact way to add specific columns by position. It works, but it is fragile if column order ever changes, so in production code I would probably use `select()` plus column names instead.
-   The final `filter(TOTAL > 0)` removes subzones with no residents, which would otherwise be undefined on the triangle.

## Building a static ternary plot

```{r}
#| label: ternary-static
#| eval: false
ggtern(data = agpop_mutated, 
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Population structure, 2018") +
  theme_rgbw()
```

`theme_rgbw()` gives the triangle a clean white background with light gridlines, which I find much easier to read than the default. The `ggtern` API uses `x`, `y`, and `z` instead of two axes, but otherwise everything composes like normal ggplot layers.

## Building an interactive ternary plot

For the interactive version I switch to `plot_ly()` with `type = "scatterternary"`. The interactivity is genuinely useful here because each point is a planning subzone, and being able to hover to identify which subzone has an unusually old or young profile is much more efficient than reading a legend.

```{r}
#| label: ternary-interactive
#| eval: false
label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt, 
    x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper", 
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2
  )
}

axis <- function(txt) {
  list(
    title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10)
  )
}

ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"), 
  baxis = axis("Active"), 
  caxis = axis("Old")
)

plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated, 
  a = ~YOUNG, 
  b = ~ACTIVE, 
  c = ~OLD, 
  color = I("black"), 
  type = "scatterternary"
) %>%
  layout(
    annotations = label("Ternary Markers"), 
    ternary = ternaryAxes
  )
```

## My takeaway

Ternary plots are surprisingly powerful when the data really is compositional. And They only make sense when the three parts logically sum to a whole. For Singapore's age structure, the cluster of points pulled towards the "active" corner tells the demographic story in one glance.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 2: Visual Correlation Analysis

## Why bother visualising correlations?

When I have ten or more numerical variables, eyeballing a correlation table of numbers is exhausting and error-prone. A visual correlation matrix solves three problems at once:

1.  It reveals the **pairwise relationships** between variables at a glance.
2.  It feeds into downstream analyses such as regression and factor analysis.
3.  It acts as a **diagnostic** for multicollinearity before I run any model.

For this section I use the Wine Quality dataset, with 11 numerical chemical properties across roughly 6,500 wines.

## Packages

```{r}
#| label: corr-setup
pacman::p_load(corrplot, ggstatsplot, tidyverse)
```

## Importing the data

```{r}
#| label: corr-import
#| eval: false
wine <- read_csv("data/wine_quality.csv")
```

## Method 1: Base R `pairs()`

The simplest scatterplot matrix uses base R's `pairs()`:

```{r}
#| label: pairs-basic
#| eval: false
pairs(wine[, 2:12])
```

Since a correlation matrix is symmetric, showing both halves is redundant. I prefer to show only the lower half:

```{r}
#| label: pairs-lower
#| eval: false
pairs(wine[, 2:12], upper.panel = NULL)
```

And to be smarter still, I can replace one half with the actual correlation coefficients, sized by magnitude:

```{r}
#| label: panel-cor
#| eval: false
panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits = 2, prefix = "", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr")
  on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits = digits)[1]
  txt <- paste(prefix, txt, sep = "")
  if (missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8 / strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}

pairs(wine[, 2:12], upper.panel = panel.cor)
```

::: callout-note
## When `pairs()` breaks down

With 6,500 observations the scatter clouds become huge inky blobs and stop being readable. That is exactly the limitation that motivates the corrgram approach below.
:::

## Method 2: `ggcorrmat()` from ggstatsplot

`ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat()` is my favourite for **reporting**, because it produces a publication-ready figure with statistical annotation baked in:

```{r}
#| label: ggcorrmat
#| eval: false
ggstatsplot::ggcorrmat(
  data = wine, 
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black", 
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
  subtitle = "Four pairs are not significant at p < 0.05"
)
```

`hc.order = TRUE` reorders the variables by hierarchical clustering, similar variables end up next to each other, so the block structure of the correlation matrix becomes visually obvious.

For grouped comparisons (red wine vs white wine), I can use `grouped_ggcorrmat()`:

```{r}
#| label: grouped-ggcorrmat
#| eval: false
grouped_ggcorrmat(
  data = wine,
  cor.vars = 1:11,
  grouping.var = type,
  type = "robust",
  p.adjust.method = "holm",
  plotgrid.args = list(ncol = 2),
  ggcorrplot.args = list(outline.color = "black", 
                         hc.order = TRUE,
                         tl.cex = 10),
  annotation.args = list(
    tag_levels = "a",
    title = "Correlogram for wine dataset",
    caption = "Dataset: UCI Machine Learning Repository"
  )
)
```

## Method 3: `corrplot` for fine control

Where `ggcorrmat` is opinionated, `corrplot` is endlessly customisable. First I compute the correlation matrix explicitly:

```{r}
#| label: corrplot-cor
#| eval: false
wine.cor <- cor(wine[, 1:11])
corrplot(wine.cor)
```

The default uses **circles**, with **blue for positive** and **red for negative** correlations, and **colour intensity for magnitude**.

### Changing the visual encoding

Seven `method` options exist (`circle`, `square`, `ellipse`, `number`, `shade`, `color`, `pie`). Ellipses are my go-to because the tilt of the ellipse encodes the sign of the correlation visually:

```{r}
#| label: corrplot-ellipse
#| eval: false
corrplot(wine.cor, 
         method = "ellipse",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black")
```

### Mixed layout: glyphs on one side, numbers on the other

This is the design I usually settle on for final reports because it captures both shape and exact magnitude in one figure:

```{r}
#| label: corrplot-mixed
#| eval: false
corrplot.mixed(wine.cor, 
               lower = "ellipse", 
               upper = "number",
               tl.pos = "lt",
               diag = "l",
               tl.col = "black")
```

### Significance testing overlay

Correlation strength is not the whole story.

```{r}
#| label: corrplot-sig
#| eval: false
wine.sig = cor.mtest(wine.cor, conf.level = .95)

corrplot(wine.cor,
         method = "number",
         type = "lower",
         diag = FALSE,
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.srt = 45,
         p.mat = wine.sig$p,
         sig.level = .05)
```

Non-significant cells get crossed out, which is much more informative than presenting raw r-values as if they were all equally trustworthy.

### Reordering with hierarchical clustering

Reordering is where corrgrams really earn their keep. By default the variables appear in whatever order the dataframe had them, which is rarely the most informative arrangement:

```{r}
#| label: corrplot-hclust
#| eval: false
corrplot(wine.cor, 
         method = "ellipse", 
         tl.pos = "lt",
         tl.col = "black",
         order = "hclust",
         hclust.method = "ward.D",
         addrect = 3)
```

The `addrect = 3` argument draws boxes around three clusters.

## My takeaway

For exploration I reach for `corrplot` because of its flexibility. For formal reports I prefer `ggcorrmat` because the statistical annotation is built in. Either way, **reordering by clustering** is the single most underrated technique here, it transforms a chaotic matrix into a structured one.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 3: Heatmaps for Multivariate Patterns

## What heatmaps are good at

A heatmap is essentially a coloured table. Rows are observations, columns are variables, and each cell's colour encodes a value. The reason this works so well is that human vision is very good at picking out **horizontal bands of similar colour** (similar observations) and **vertical bands** (related variables). Adding dendrograms on the sides lets the algorithm reorder both axes so those bands actually emerge.

For this section I use the World Happiness 2018 dataset.

## Packages

```{r}
#| label: heatmap-setup
pacman::p_load(seriation, dendextend, heatmaply, tidyverse)
```

## Importing and preparing the data

```{r}
#| label: heatmap-import
#| eval: false
wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")
row.names(wh) <- wh$Country
```

Setting row names to country names is essential, otherwise the heatmap labels are useless row numbers. The next step is converting to a matrix because most heatmap functions require that input class:

```{r}
#| label: heatmap-matrix
#| eval: false
wh_matrix <- data.matrix(wh)
```

## Static heatmap with `heatmap()`

The base R `heatmap()` is a fine starting point:

```{r}
#| label: heatmap-static
#| eval: false
wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)
```

With `Rowv = NA` and `Colv = NA`, both dendrograms are switched off, so I see the raw matrix with no reordering. That is rarely what I want, but it is useful as a baseline.

### The scaling problem

If I plot the matrix without scaling, one variable (Happiness Score) dominates because its raw values are much larger than the others. Everything else looks washed out. Column-wise scaling fixes this:

```{r}
#| label: heatmap-scaled
#| eval: false
wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      scale = "column",
                      cexRow = 0.6, 
                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))
```

`scale = "column"` z-scores each column, so every variable now contributes on equal footing. **This is a small change with an enormous effect on interpretability**.

## Interactive heatmap with `heatmaply`

`heatmaply` produces interactive heatmaps that render as standalone HTML.

```{r}
#| label: heatmaply-basic
#| eval: false
heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)])
```

### Three data transformation methods

`heatmaply` offers three transformations, and choosing between them is more than cosmetic:

```{r}
#| label: heatmaply-scale
#| eval: false
# Scaling: assumes (or imposes) normal-like distributions
heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)],
          scale = "column")

# Normalising: maps to [0, 1] using min and max
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))

# Percentising: maps each value to its empirical percentile
heatmaply(percentize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))
```

-   **Scale** when variables are roughly normal and outliers are meaningful.
-   **Normalise** when distributions are skewed but I still want raw shape preserved.
-   **Percentise** when I care about relative rank rather than absolute values, which is often the case for indicators across countries.

### Picking the right clustering setup

Choosing a clustering method by intuition feels arbitrary. The `dendextend` package lets me pick statistically:

```{r}
#| label: dendextend-pick
#| eval: false
wh_d <- dist(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]), method = "euclidean")
dend_expend(wh_d)[[3]]
```

The output table shows an `optim` score for each clustering method. The highest score wins, which in this dataset is `average`. Then I find the best number of clusters:

```{r}
#| label: dendextend-k
#| eval: false
wh_clust <- hclust(wh_d, method = "average")
num_k <- find_k(wh_clust)
plot(num_k)
```

Putting both choices into the heatmap call:

```{r}
#| label: heatmaply-clustered
#| eval: false
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "average",
          k_row = 3)
```

### Seriation: getting the row order right

Clustering tells me the *tree structure*, but it does not tell me how to *rotate* the branches. Two valid orderings of the same cluster tree can produce very different-looking heatmaps. The `seriate = "OLO"` (Optimal Leaf Ordering) argument fixes this by minimising the sum of dissimilarities between adjacent rows:

```{r}
#| label: heatmaply-olo
#| eval: false
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          seriate = "OLO")
```

### Final polished version

Putting it all together with thoughtful styling:

```{r}
#| label: heatmaply-final
#| eval: false
heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          Colv = NA,
          seriate = "none",
          colors = Blues,
          k_row = 5,
          margins = c(NA, 200, 60, NA),
          fontsize_row = 4,
          fontsize_col = 5,
          main = "World Happiness Score and Variables by Country, 2018",
          xlab = "World Happiness Indicators",
          ylab = "World Countries")
```

## My takeaway

Heatmaps reward attention to three details: **scaling**, **clustering choice**, and **leaf ordering**. Get all three right and the patterns essentially announce themselves. Get any one wrong and the chart can mislead.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 4: Parallel Coordinates Plots

## What parallel coordinates plots do differently

If a scatterplot matrix shows me *pairwise* relationships, a parallel coordinates plot shows me **whole observations as lines** across many variables. Each vertical axis is one variable. Each line is one observation, threading through all the variables in sequence. This is uniquely good at:

-   Identifying clusters of similarly-behaved observations.
-   Spotting outliers that take an unusual path across variables.
-   Characterising customer segments or country groupings.

## Packages

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-setup
pacman::p_load(GGally, parallelPlot, tidyverse)
```

## Importing the data

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-import
#| eval: false
wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")
```

## Static plot with `ggparcoord()`

A bare-minimum plot:

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-basic
#| eval: false
ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12))
```

This is honestly not very useful on its own. Without colour or scaling, all lines pile on top of each other. The makeover below is where it becomes informative:

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-improved
#| eval: false
ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12), 
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE, 
           title = "Parallel Coordinates Plot of World Happiness Variables")
```

The four arguments that transform this chart:

-   `groupColumn = 2` colours lines by Region, which is where regional patterns start to emerge.
-   `scale = "uniminmax"` puts every axis on a 0-to-1 range, so axes are comparable.
-   `alphaLines = 0.2` makes lines semi-transparent, so overlapping bundles look denser. This is essential for any dataset above maybe 30 observations.
-   `boxplot = TRUE` overlays a boxplot per axis, summarising distribution shape.

### Faceting by region

For comparing regions head-to-head, faceting works beautifully:

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-facet
#| eval: false
ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12), 
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE, 
           title = "Parallel Coordinates by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

The `angle = 30, hjust = 1` combination rotates and right-aligns axis labels so they do not overlap the plot, which is a small detail that makes a big visual difference.

## Interactive plot with `parallelPlot`

For exploratory work, interactivity is genuinely useful here because clicking on an axis recolours all lines by that variable, which is the fastest way to ask "what drives this variable?":

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-interactive
#| eval: false
wh <- wh %>%
  select("Happiness score", c(7:12))

parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle = TRUE)
```

### Customising colour and adding histograms

```{r}
#| label: parcoord-histo
#| eval: false
parallelPlot(wh,
             continuousCS = "YlOrRd",
             rotateTitle = TRUE)

histoVisibility <- rep(TRUE, ncol(wh))
parallelPlot(wh,
             rotateTitle = TRUE,
             histoVisibility = histoVisibility)
```

The histograms along each axis are an underrated feature, because they show me the marginal distribution of each variable without leaving the chart.

## My takeaway

Parallel coordinates plots have a steep aesthetic learning curve. The defaults look terrible. But once I add scaling, transparency, grouping colour, and boxplots, they become one of the most informative views possible for medium-sized multivariate datasets. The interactive version is what I would actually use in a live exploratory session.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Part 5: Treemap for Hierarchical Composition

## When to use a treemap

Treemaps shine for **hierarchical part-to-whole** data where I care about both the level of the hierarchy and the relative sizes of leaves. Classic use cases:

-   Budget breakdowns by department and team.
-   Stock market sectors and constituent companies.
-   Property transactions by region, planning area, and project (which is exactly what I do here).

The two things I will always encode are **rectangle size** (a magnitude) and **rectangle colour** (a separate metric, often a rate or median).

## Packages

```{r}
#| label: treemap-setup
pacman::p_load(treemap, treemapify, tidyverse)
```

## Data wrangling

The raw REALIS 2018 dataset is at the transaction level. A treemap needs aggregated values, so I group and summarise:

```{r}
#| label: treemap-import
#| eval: false
realis2018 <- read_csv("data/realis2018.csv")

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>% 
  group_by(`Project Name`, `Planning Region`, 
           `Planning Area`, `Property Type`, 
           `Type of Sale`) %>%
  summarise(`Total Unit Sold` = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE), 
            `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Transacted Price` = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))
```

Notice the pipe pattern: group, then summarise. This is one of those dplyr workflows that you internalise once and then use forever. The `na.rm = TRUE` is essential because any missing value in a column would otherwise propagate to the whole summary.

Filter to a single property type and sale type to keep the example focused:

```{r}
#| label: treemap-filter
#| eval: false
realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(`Property Type` == "Condominium", 
         `Type of Sale` == "Resale")
```

## Static treemap with `treemap()`

The three arguments that do most of the work are `index`, `vSize`, and `vColor`:

```{r}
#| label: treemap-basic
#| eval: false
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
```

::: callout-warning
## A subtle gotcha

By default, `vColor` does not actually map to a colour gradient. Without setting `type`, `treemap()` assumes `type = "index"` and colours rectangles by hierarchy category instead of by the numeric variable.
:::

### Fixing the colour mapping

To actually map colour to the numeric variable, I need to set `type`:

```{r}
#| label: treemap-value
#| eval: false
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "value",
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
```

### "value" vs "manual": what is the difference?

This tripped me up at first. Both use `vColor`, but the mapping logic differs:

-   `type = "value"` treats the palette as **diverging around zero**. Negative values pull towards the left-end colour, positive values pull towards the right. Good for data that can be negative.
-   `type = "manual"` maps **min(values) to the left end and max(values) to the right end**, with mean in the middle. Good for strictly-positive data.

For unit prices, which are all positive, `manual` with a sequential palette like `Blues` is the right combination:

```{r}
#| label: treemap-manual
#| eval: false
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "manual",
        palette = "Blues", 
        title = "Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")
```

### Layout algorithms

`treemap` supports two algorithms:

-   **squarified**: optimises aspect ratios so rectangles are close to squares. Ignores sort order.
-   **pivotSize** (default): respects a `sortID` ordering at the cost of slightly worse aspect ratios.

```{r}
#| label: treemap-algo
#| eval: false
treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "manual",
        palette = "Blues", 
        algorithm = "pivotSize",
        sortID = "Median Transacted Price",
        title = "Sorted by Median Transacted Price")
```

I usually pick `squarified` for purely visual comparison and `pivotSize` with `sortID` when there is a meaningful order to encode.

## Treemap with `treemapify` (ggplot grammar)

`treemapify` lets me build treemaps inside ggplot, which is great when I want to combine with other ggplot features:

```{r}
#| label: treemapify-basic
#| eval: false
ggplot(data = realis2018_selected, 
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) + 
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "gray20")
```

The two `*_border` layers draw nested borders for each hierarchy level, which makes the structure visually obvious. I find this version cleaner than the `treemap()` output for final reports.

## Interactive treemap with `d3treeR`

For drilldown exploration, the interactive `d3treeR` package wraps a `treemap` object into a clickable d3 visualisation:

```{r}
#| label: d3tree-install
#| eval: false
# install.packages("devtools")
# library(devtools)
# install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")
library(d3treeR)
```

```{r}
#| label: d3tree-build
#| eval: false
tm <- treemap(realis2018_summarised,
        index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area"),
        vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
        vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "value",
        title = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2018",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)")

d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")
```

Clicking a region zooms into that region's planning areas. For a stakeholder-facing dashboard this is genuinely useful, because it lets the viewer explore at their own pace instead of forcing one fixed view.

## My takeaway

Treemaps are easy to make wrong and reasonably easy to make right. The two non-negotiables are: (1) set `type` explicitly so colour actually maps to your numeric variable, and (2) use a sequential palette when values are strictly positive. Everything else is polish.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Closing Reflections

| Chart type | Best for | Watch out for |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Ternary plot** | Three-part compositions | Only works if parts sum to a whole |
| **Correlation visualisation** | Pairwise numerical relationships | Reorder by clustering, always |
| **Heatmap** | Multivariate patterns and clusters | Scale your data, choose seriation carefully |
| **Parallel coordinates** | Tracing observations across variables | Use transparency and grouping colour |
| **Treemap** | Hierarchical part-to-whole | Set `type` explicitly, pick palette to match data sign |

What unifies them is that each one is a different answer to the same question: **how do I see structure in a dataset that has more than two or three dimensions?**
